intro: Geneva eats with one foot in French Savoie and the other in Swiss Romandie. Long Alpine lunches, fondue suppers, lake-fish tartares, and serious wine from canton-grown Chasselas and Gamay define the city. Prices are steep, but the quality—and the precision—is uniformly excellent.

sections.title: The Essentials
sections.content:
**Mornings** belong to neighbourhood bakeries. Order a **croissant au beurre** and a **café crème** at the bar of a café or boulangerie—standing is cheaper than sitting, and Genevans treat the morning espresso as a quick ritual rather than a sit-down.

**Lunch** is the best meal-value of the day. Many bistros offer a **plat du jour** for CHF 22–28 and a two-course **menu du midi** for CHF 28–38—a fraction of dinner prices. Kitchens serve noon to 2pm sharp; arriving at 1:50pm risks being turned away.

**Dinner** runs from 7pm and most kitchens close by 9:30pm or 10pm—earlier than Paris or Madrid. Reserve anywhere you specifically want to eat; the better Carouge, Eaux-Vives, and Pâquis tables fill days in advance.

**Tipping**: service is included by Swiss law. Round up or add 5% for exceptional service—nothing more is expected.

sections.title: What to Eat
sections.content:
**Fondue & raclette** — The Geneva canton's signature winter dishes. Fondue moitié-moitié (Gruyère + Vacherin Fribourgeois) is the classic; raclette is melted wheel-cheese scraped over potatoes and pickles. Eat at Café du Soleil, Les Armures, or a Carouge bistro between October and April.

**Filets de perche** — Lake Geneva perch fillets, lightly fried in butter with lemon and parsley. The defining lakefront dish; best at lakeside terraces in Pâquis, Eaux-Vives, and Hermance.

**Longeole** — A Geneva PGI sausage flavoured with fennel seed, slow-cooked with cardoons (cardons, a thistle-like winter vegetable). Heart of a Geneva Sunday lunch.

**Chocolate** — Geneva is one of the world's chocolate capitals. Auer, Du Rhône, Stettler, and Sprüngli (national) are essentials. Try a single bar from a small chocolatier and a hot chocolate at Auer.

**Tarte aux pruneaux** — Plum tart eaten traditionally on **Jeûne Genevois** (Thursday after the first Sunday of September). Available year-round at the city's best pâtisseries.

sections.title: Where to Drink
sections.content:
**Geneva wine** is a revelation. Mandement (Satigny, Russin, Dardagny) is the canton's vineyard heartland—Chasselas whites, Gamay reds, and the spicy Gamaret are exceptional and almost never exported. Order them by the glass at any bistro and visit the vineyards in season.

**Apéro** is the lakefront ritual. From 6pm, terraces along Quai du Mont-Blanc and Quai Gustave-Ador fill with people nursing a glass of Chasselas, a kir (white wine with cassis), or a Genevan craft beer (BFM, Trois Dames).

**Wine bars** — Le Verre à Monique in Carouge, La Cave de la Vigneronne in Eaux-Vives, and La Bottega in the Old Town pour the best of Swiss and natural French wines with charcuterie boards.

**Cocktails** — The Bottle Brothers, Atelier Cocktail Club, and the rooftop bars of the Mandarin Oriental and Hôtel Métropole lead the scene. Expect CHF 18–24 per drink.

**Coffee** culture is closer to Italian than Parisian—short, strong, and never a cappuccino after 11am.

sections.title: Neighbourhoods for Food
sections.content:
**Carouge (Sardinian-grid 'little Italy')** — The city's most charming food district. Bistros, wine bars, gelato, pizzerias, and the Marché de Carouge twice weekly. Tram 12 from the centre.

**Pâquis** — Cosmopolitan and late-night. Fondue institutions, Lebanese mezze, Ethiopian, Thai, and excellent lakefront brasseries. Walk from Cornavin.

**Old Town & Place du Bourg-de-Four** — Traditional Geneva: Les Armures, Café Papon, Restaurant du Palais de Justice. Best for fondue with cobblestone atmosphere.

**Eaux-Vives** — Quieter lakefront luxury, polished fine-dining, and a strong neighbourhood of natural-wine bars and seasonal bistros.

**Plainpalais & Quartier des Bains** — Student-and-creative energy; weekend Plainpalais market (Geneva's biggest), wine bars open late on Thursdays, casual neo-bistros.

sections.title: Practical Tips
sections.content:
**Reservations** — Essential for dinner Wed–Sat at top bistros. Use the restaurant's own site or LaFourchette / TheFork for last-minute spots.

**Water** — Ask for **'une carafe d'eau, s'il vous plaît.'** Geneva tap water is exceptional and free; bottled mineral water is a needless tourist tax.

**Bread** — Arrives automatically and is free. Use it; it's the carrier for sauces, raclette, and fondue scraping.

**Cards** — Visa/Mastercard tap-to-pay accepted nearly everywhere; Amex patchier in small bistros. Currency is CHF, never euros (rates near the airport and station are punishing).

**Cheese & chocolate to take home** — Buy at the source: Bruand or Tour de l'Île for cheese; Auer or Du Rhône for chocolate. Vacuum-sealed cheese travels fine; chocolate prefers under 18°C.

**The check** — Won't arrive unprompted. Say **'l'addition, s'il vous plaît.'** Lingering after dinner is welcome; turn-over pressure is rare outside the busiest spots.
